title: Chenolea eriophora
taxonomy: Chenolea eriophora is a genus of flowering plants in the family Rubiaceae. Chenolea eriophora is native to Australia and has been classified within the family of flowering plants known as Rubiaceae. This classification places Chenolea eriophora in the order Gentianales, which is a large and diverse order of flowering plants. The family Rubiaceae, to which Chenolea eriophora belongs, is commonly known as the madder family. Chenolea eriophora's classification is further divided into its taxonomic ranks, including kingdom, phylum, class, order, family, genus, and species.

parts: Chenolea eriophora, also known as the Queensland daisy bush, has been traditionally used in Australian Indigenous medicine. Its leaves and stems are reportedly used for medicinal purposes. The plant's leaves are said to have anti-inflammatory properties and are used to treat various ailments, including skin conditions and fever. intro: Chenolea eriophora, also known as Sea Lavender or Chenopodium, is a coastal plant species native to Australia, characterized by its prostrate habit and small, star-shaped flowers. The plant has been traditionally used for its medicinal properties, offering health benefits such as anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial effects, as well as potential treatments for digestive issues and wounds. Chenopodium has been found to contain bioactive constituents including flavonoids, saponins, and phenolic acids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, Chenopodium is commonly prepared as a tea or infusion, or used in salves and ointments to take advantage of its wound-healing and antimicrobial properties.
